votes.reason: The Python code correctly checks that the result list is sorted, contains no duplicates, and has the exact same set of elements as the input list, which perfectly matches the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly checks that the result list is sorted, contains no duplicates, and has the exact same set of elements as the input list, which perfectly matches the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly checks the three conditions from the Coq spec: (1) sorted in non-decreasing order via pairwise comparison, (2) no duplicates by comparing length to set size, (3) same elements membership via set equality. For concrete inputs, these checks produce the same boolean judgment as the Coq specification's conjunction of Sorted, NoDup, and bidirectional In membership.

The Python code correctly checks the three conditions from the Coq spec: (1) sorted in non-decreasing order via pairwise comparison, (2) no duplicates by comparing length to set size, (3) same elements membership via set equality. For concrete finite lists of integers, these checks produce the same boolean judgment as the Coq specification's conjunction of Sorted, NoDup, and bidirectional membership.

The Python code checks the same three conditions as the Coq spec: (1) sorted in non-decreasing order via pairwise comparison, (2) no duplicates via set comparison, (3) same elements membership via set equality. For finite lists with concrete inputs, these produce the same judgments as the Coq predicates Sorted Z.le, NoDup, and the bidirectional In membership.

Both require res to be nondecreasing, contain no duplicates, and have exactly the same elements as l (ignoring multiplicities). Python ints and set equality match Coq Z and In semantics, so results align on all inputs.
It checks nondecreasing order (<=), no duplicates via set size, and equality of element sets, matching Sorted Z.le, NoDup, and membership equivalence for Z lists.
